title: Cyber Security Act 2023 Bangladesh – What Changed from DSA? Your Rights Explained
metaTitle: Cyber Security Act 2023 Bangladesh – DSA vs CSA: Full Legal Breakdown & Your Rights
metaDescription: Bangladesh replaced the Digital Security Act with the Cyber Security Act 2023. What changed? New offences, penalties, bail rules & your legal rights if accused.
heroIntro: In 2023, Bangladesh replaced the widely criticised Digital Security Act 2018 (DSA) with the Cyber Security Act 2023 (CSA). This complete guide explains what changed, what stayed the same, the new offences and penalties, and — crucially — what your legal rights are if you are accused or arrested under the CSA 2023.

quickAnswer.heading: ⚡ Quick Answer: Cyber Security Act 2023 vs DSA
points: DSA 2018 was repealed and replaced by the Cyber Security Act 2023
Key change: Some non-bailable offences from DSA are now bailable under CSA
Key concern: Most substantive offences (defamation, hacking, propaganda) remain — just renamed
Section 21 (harming liberation war spirit) was removed from CSA
New CSA is still controversial — critics say core human rights concerns remain
If arrested under CSA 2023: Call a lawyer immediately — bail conditions differ from DSA

toc: Offences Under the Cyber Security Act 2023
Bail Under the Cyber Security Act 2023
Your Rights if Accused Under CSA 2023
How to File a Complaint Under CSA 2023
Is the CSA 2023 Better Than the DSA?

sections.content:
The Digital Security Act 2018 (DSA) was one of Bangladesh's most controversial laws. Key criticisms included:

  • Abuse against journalists and activists: Dozens of journalists, bloggers, and social media users were arrested for online posts under DSA sections 21, 25, 28, and 29
  • Non-bailable offences: Many DSA offences were non-bailable, meaning accused persons could be detained for extended periods without bail
  • Overbroad definitions: Terms like 'propaganda against liberation war' and 'hurting religious sentiments' were vaguely defined
  • International pressure: The United Nations, EU, and press freedom organisations repeatedly called for DSA's repeal

In August 2023, the Bangladesh government enacted the Cyber Security Act 2023 — officially replacing the DSA 2018, though critics argue the substantive changes are more cosmetic than fundamental.

Feature DSA 2018 CSA 2023
Section 21 (Liberation War propaganda) Included — 14 yrs to life ✅ Removed
Section 25 (offensive/false information) Non-bailable, 3 yrs ⚠️ Retained, now bailable
Section 28 (religious sentiment) Non-bailable, 5–10 yrs ⚠️ Retained with modifications
Section 29 (defamation) Non-bailable, 3–5 yrs ⚠️ Retained, now bailable
Section 31 (creating unrest) Non-bailable, 7 yrs ⚠️ Retained as non-bailable
Hacking/unauthorized access Non-bailable, 7–14 yrs ⚠️ Retained with similar penalties
Police arrest powers Arrest without warrant ⚠️ Largely retained

sections.h2: Offences Under the Cyber Security Act 2023
sections.content:
The CSA 2023 covers a wide range of digital offences:

Offences Against Digital Infrastructure

  • Illegal access/hacking to computer systems, servers, or networks
  • Disruption of digital services (DDoS attacks)
  • Illegal software transmission — viruses, malware, ransomware

Offences Related to Digital Content

  • Publication of false or offensive information online
  • Defamation through digital means
  • Hurting religious sentiments via digital content
  • Creating social unrest or disorder through online content

Financial Cyber Offences

  • Online fraud and e-commerce cheating
  • Identity theft — impersonating others online
  • Mobile banking fraud (bKash, Nagad)

Privacy and Data Offences

  • Cy blackmail and sextortion — sharing intimate images without consent
  • Cyberstalking and online harassment
  • Illegal interception of digital communications

Offence Max Imprisonment Bailable?
Illegal system access (hacking) 7 years (repeat: 14 yrs) No
Publishing false/offensive information 3 years Yes
Defamation online 3 years Yes
Hurting religious sentiments 5–10 years Case by case
Creating social unrest online 7 years No
Cyberstalking / online harassment 5 years Yes
Sharing intimate images without consent 7 years No
Identity theft 5 years Yes

sections.h2: Bail Under the Cyber Security Act 2023
sections.content:
One of the most significant changes in the CSA 2023 compared to the DSA 2018 is the reclassification of several offences from non-bailable to bailable:

Now Bailable Under CSA 2023 (Previously Non-Bailable Under DSA)

  • Defamation through digital means (Section 29 equivalent)
  • Publishing offensive/false information (Section 25 equivalent)
  • Cyberstalking and online harassment
  • Identity theft

Remains Non-Bailable Under CSA 2023

  • Illegal system access/hacking (serious cases)
  • Sharing intimate images without consent
  • Creating social unrest through online content
  • Attacks on critical digital infrastructure

For bailable offences under CSA 2023, you have the right to bail from the Magistrate Court. For non-bailable offences, you must argue before the Sessions Court or High Court. A cyber law lawyer in Dhaka should be contacted immediately upon arrest.

sections.h2: Your Rights if Accused Under CSA 2023
sections.content:
If you are accused, arrested, or charged under the Cyber Security Act 2023, these are your constitutional and legal rights:

  • Right to know the reason for arrest: Police must inform you of the specific section you are being arrested under
  • Right to a lawyer: You have the right to consult and be represented by a lawyer from the moment of arrest
  • Right to be produced before a Magistrate within 24 hours: Police cannot detain you beyond 24 hours without a court order
  • Right to bail: For bailable offences — bail is a right. For non-bailable — apply immediately to the Sessions Court or High Court
  • Right to remain silent: You are not required to make any statement to police. Anything you say can and will be used against you
  • Right to challenge remand: If police seek remand (custody extension), your lawyer can oppose and challenge this in court

Do not speak to police without a lawyer present. Many CSA cases weaken significantly once a qualified criminal lawyer challenges the remand application and the evidence chain. Contact us immediately if you or a family member has been arrested under the CSA 2023.

sections.h2: How to File a Complaint Under CSA 2023
sections.content:
If you are a victim of a cyber crime under CSA 2023, here is how to report it:

  1. Option 1 — Police Cyber Crime Unit: File a complaint at the nearest police station or directly at the CID Cyber Police Centre, Dhaka. Preserve digital evidence (screenshots, chat logs, account details) before reporting.
  2. Option 2 — Bangladesh Police Cyber Crime Complaint Portal: Report online at the Bangladesh police cybercrime portal (cyber.police.gov.bd).
  3. Option 3 — Cyber Tribunal: For serious cases, a complaint petition can be filed directly before the Cyber Tribunal established under the CSA. Your lawyer can file this directly without police complaint first.

Evidence you should preserve: screenshots with timestamps, URLs, account IDs, any transaction records, communications with the accused, witness statements. A cyber crime lawyer in Dhaka can advise on evidence preservation and collection.

sections.h2: Is the CSA 2023 Better Than the DSA?
sections.content:
The answer is: marginally better in limited ways, but fundamentally similar in concerns.

Improvements in CSA 2023:

  • Section 21 (Liberation War propaganda) — the most heavily abused DSA section — has been removed
  • Several offences reclassified from non-bailable to bailable (significant practical improvement for defendants)
  • Some ambiguous language tightened

Remaining concerns in CSA 2023:

  • Core 'chilling effect' offences remain — defamation, offensive content, religious sentiment are still criminalised online
  • Police can still arrest without warrant for many offences
  • Vague definitions of 'social unrest' and 'offensive content' remain
  • Critics including Reporters Without Borders maintain that fundamental press freedom concerns persist

faqs.question: Is the Digital Security Act still in force in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: No. The Digital Security Act 2018 (DSA) was officially repealed and replaced by the Cyber Security Act 2023 (CSA). All new cases are now filed under the CSA 2023. Cases already pending under DSA continue under the old law.

faqs.question: What is the most important change in the Cyber Security Act 2023?
faqs.answer: The most significant change is the reclassification of several key offences from non-bailable (DSA) to bailable (CSA). This means accused persons for defamation, offensive content, and cyberstalking now have a right to bail from Magistrate Court — previously they could be detained without bail.

faqs.question: Can a journalist be arrested under the Cyber Security Act 2023?
faqs.answer: Yes. While Section 21 (the most widely abused DSA provision) has been removed, sections covering defamation, offensive content, and creating social unrest remain in the CSA 2023 and continue to pose legal risks for journalists, bloggers, and social media users.

faqs.question: Is hacking still illegal under the Cyber Security Act 2023?
faqs.answer: Yes. Unauthorized access to computer systems, networks, or digital devices remains a serious criminal offence under CSA 2023, punishable by up to 7 years imprisonment (14 years for repeat offences). It remains non-bailable.

faqs.question: What should I do if arrested under the Cyber Security Act 2023?
faqs.answer: Do not make any statements to police without a lawyer present. Exercise your constitutional right to legal representation immediately. Call a criminal lawyer in Dhaka who handles cyber cases — for bailable offences, file for bail at the Magistrate Court immediately; for non-bailable offences, file at the Sessions Court or High Court.
